([Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language "Māori language"): *Te Whare Wānanga o Waitaha*) is a [public research university](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") based in [Christchurch](/wiki/Christchurch "Christchurch"), New Zealand. It was founded in 1873 as **Canterbury College**, the first constituent college of the [University of New Zealand](/wiki/University_of_New_Zealand "University of New Zealand"). It is New Zealand's [second-oldest university](/wiki/List_of_oldest_universities_in_continuous_operation#Oceania "List of oldest universities in continuous operation"), after the [University of Otago](/wiki/University_of_Otago "University of Otago"), which was founded four years earlier, in 1869.

Over the period from 1961 to 1974, the university campus relocated from the centre of the city to its much larger current site in the suburb of Ilam. 1973 saw the university celebrate its centenary, during which the [neo-Gothic](/wiki/Neo-Gothic "Neo-Gothic") buildings of the old campus were gifted to the City of Christchurch, which became the site of the [Christchurch Arts Centre](/wiki/Christchurch_Arts_Centre "Christchurch Arts Centre"), a hub for arts, crafts and entertainment in Christchurch.[[

The **University of Canterbury** ([Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language "Māori language"): *Te Whare Wānanga o Waitaha*) is a [public research university](/wiki/Public_university "Public university") based in [Christchurch](/wiki/Christchurch "Christchurch"), New Zealand. It was founded in 1873 as **Canterbury College**, the first constituent college of the [University of New Zealand](/wiki/University_of_New_Zealand "University of New Zealand"). It is New Zealand's [second-oldest university](/wiki/List_of_oldest_universities_in_continuous_operation#Oceania "List of oldest universities in continuous operation"), after the [University of Otago](/wiki/University_of_Otago "University of Otago"), which was founded four years earlier, in 1869.

Its original campus was in the [Christchurch Central City](/wiki/Christchurch_Central_City "Christchurch Central City"), but in 1961 it became an independent university and began moving out of its original [neo-Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_Revival_architecture "Gothic Revival architecture") buildings, which were re-purposed as the [Christchurch Arts Centre](/wiki/Christchurch_Arts_Centre "Christchurch Arts Centre"). The move was completed on 1 May 1975[[6]](#cite_note-6) and the university now operates its main campus in the Christchurch suburb of [Ilam](/wiki/Ilam,_New_Zealand "Ilam, New Zealand").

The university offers bachelors, masters, and doctoral degrees in, among others, [Arts](/wiki/Humanities "Humanities"), [Commerce](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Commerce "Bachelor of Commerce"), Education ([physical education](/wiki/Physical_education "Physical education")), [Fine Arts](/wiki/Ilam_School_of_Fine_Arts "Ilam School of Fine Arts"), [Forestry](/wiki/Forestry "Forestry"), [Health Sciences](/wiki/Health_Sciences "Health Sciences"), [Law](/wiki/Law "Law"), [Criminal Justice](/wiki/Criminal_justice "Criminal justice"), Music, [Social Work](/wiki/Social_Work "Social Work"), [Speech and Language Pathology](/wiki/Speech_therapy "Speech therapy"), [Sports Coaching](/wiki/Sports_Coaching "Sports Coaching") and [Teaching](/wiki/Teaching "Teaching").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Canterbury&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Canterbury College, 1873–1960

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Canterbury&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Canterbury College, 1873–1960")]

On 16 June 1873, the university was founded in the centre of [Christchurch](/wiki/Christchurch "Christchurch") as Canterbury College, the first constituent college of the [University of New Zealand](/wiki/University_of_New_Zealand "University of New Zealand") and was funded by the then [Canterbury Provincial Council](/wiki/Canterbury_Province "Canterbury Province"). It became the second institution in New Zealand providing tertiary-level education (following the [University of Otago](/wiki/University_of_Otago "University of Otago"), established in 1869), and the fourth in [Australasia](/wiki/Australasia "Australasia").[[7]](#cite_note-7) It was founded on the basis of the [Oxbridge](/wiki/Oxbridge "Oxbridge") college system, but it differed from Oxbridge in that it admitted female students from its foundation. Its foundation professors arrived in 1874, namely, [Charles Cook](/wiki/Charles_Cook_(academic) "Charles Cook (academic)") (Mathematics, [University of Melbourne](/wiki/University_of_Melbourne "University of Melbourne"), [St John's College, Cambridge](/wiki/St_John%27s_College,_Cambridge "St John's College, Cambridge")), [Alexander Bickerton](/wiki/Alexander_William_Bickerton "Alexander William Bickerton") (Chemistry and Physics, [School of Mining](/wiki/Royal_School_of_Mines "Royal School of Mines"), London), and [John Macmillan Brown](/wiki/John_Macmillan_Brown "John Macmillan Brown") ([University of Glasgow](/wiki/University_of_Glasgow "University of Glasgow"), [Balliol College, Oxford](/wiki/Balliol_College,_Oxford "Balliol College, Oxford")).[[8]](#cite_note-8) A year later the first lectures began and in 1875 the first graduations took place. In 1880, [Helen Connon](/wiki/Helen_Connon "Helen Connon") was the first woman to graduate from the college, and in 1894, [Āpirana Ngata](/wiki/%C4%80pirana_Ngata "Āpirana Ngata") became the first [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people")-born student to graduate with a degree.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The School of Art was founded in 1882, followed by the faculties of Arts, Science, Commerce, and Law in 1921, and Mental, Moral, and Social Sciences in 1924. The Students' Union, now known as the University of Canterbury Students Association, was founded in 1929 operating out of the [Arts Centre of Christchurch Old Student Union Building](/wiki/Dux_de_Lux "Dux de Lux"), and the first edition of the student magazine [*Canta*](/wiki/Canta_(magazine) "Canta (magazine)") was published in 1930. In 1933, the name changed from Canterbury College to Canterbury University College.

College House, a student dormitory that maintains its old tradition by adopting the Oxbridge college system, broke away from [Christ's College](/wiki/Christ%27s_College,_Christchurch "Christ's College, Christchurch") in 1957 and relocated to the Ilam suburb of Christchurch in 1966 as a [hall of residence](/wiki/Hall_of_residence "Hall of residence") at the University of Canterbury.[[10]](#cite_note-10) In 1957 the name changed again to the University of Canterbury.[[11]](#cite_note-history-11)

### Independence, 1961–2009

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Canterbury&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Independence, 1961–2009")]

Until 1961, the university formed part of the [University of New Zealand](/wiki/University_of_New_Zealand "University of New Zealand") (UNZ), and issued degrees in its name. That year saw the dissolution of the federal system of tertiary education in New Zealand, and the University of Canterbury became an independent University awarding its own degrees. Upon the UNZ's demise, [Canterbury Agricultural College](/wiki/Lincoln_University_(New_Zealand) "Lincoln University (New Zealand)") became a constituent college of the University of Canterbury, as *Lincoln College*.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Lincoln College became independent in 1990 as a full university in its own right and is now known as [Lincoln University](/wiki/Lincoln_University_(New_Zealand) "Lincoln University (New Zealand)").[[13]](#cite_note-13)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/The_Christchurch_Art_Centre._%288588823446%29.jpg/330px-The_Christchurch_Art_Centre._%288588823446%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Christchurch_Art_Centre._(8588823446).jpg)

Old University of Canterbury campus, now the [Christchurch Arts Centre](/wiki/Christchurch_Arts_Centre "Christchurch Arts Centre").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Ivey_Hall%2C_Lincoln_University%2C_New_Zealand.jpg/330px-Ivey_Hall%2C_Lincoln_University%2C_New_Zealand.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ivey_Hall,_Lincoln_University,_New_Zealand.jpg)

[Ivey Hall](/wiki/Ivey_Hall "Ivey Hall") at Lincoln University formerly a constituent college of the University of Canterbury.

**Relocation to Ilam campus**

Over the period from 1961 to 1974, the university campus relocated from the centre of the city to its much larger current site in the suburb of Ilam. 1973 saw the university celebrate its centenary, during which the [neo-Gothic](/wiki/Neo-Gothic "Neo-Gothic") buildings of the old campus were gifted to the City of Christchurch, which became the site of the [Christchurch Arts Centre](/wiki/Christchurch_Arts_Centre "Christchurch Arts Centre"), a hub for arts, crafts and entertainment in Christchurch.[[14]](#cite_note-14) 1974 also marked the opening of the James Hight Library, which at the time, was New Zealand's largest university building. Ilam's three university halls of residence were renamed University Hall in 1974, and the student dormitory was used as the [Athletes Village](/wiki/Athletes_village "Athletes village") dormitory for the [1974 British Commonwealth Games](/wiki/1974_British_Commonwealth_Games "1974 British Commonwealth Games") hosted in Christchurch.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

In 2004, the university underwent restructuring into four Colleges and a School of Law, administering a number of schools and departments (though a number of departments have involvement in cross-teaching in numerous academic faculties). For many years the university worked closely with the Christchurch College of Education, leading to a full merger in 2007, establishing a fifth College.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

### Post-earthquakes, 2010–2022

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Canterbury&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Post-earthquakes, 2010–2022")]

Main articles: [2010 Canterbury earthquake](/wiki/2010_Canterbury_earthquake "2010 Canterbury earthquake") and [2011 Christchurch earthquake](/wiki/2011_Christchurch_earthquake "2011 Christchurch earthquake")

On 4 September 2010 at 4:35 am local time an earthquake struck the South Island of New Zealand with a moment magnitude of 7.1 several aftershocks followed the main event, the strongest of which was a magnitude 6.3 shock known as the Christchurch earthquake that occurred nearly six months later on 22 February 2011.[[17]](#cite_note-17) Although there was no serious injuries to staff or students on campus and only minor damage to buildings, the initial quake closed the university for a week, and the library was shut for months while shelves were repaired and half a million books placed back on shelves.[[18]](#cite_note-18) The [Student Volunteer Army](/wiki/Student_Volunteer_Army "Student Volunteer Army") was a group of around 10,000 university students and others who worked over a period of months to help clean up liquefaction.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

In the months following the earthquake, the university lost 25 per cent of its first-year students and 8 per cent of continuing students. The number of international students, who pay much higher fees and were a major source of revenue, dropped by 30 per cent.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) In October 2011, staff were encouraged to take voluntary redundancies.[[22]](#cite_note-News.com.au_1226158056545-22) As well in September 2011, plans were announced to demolish some University buildings that were damaged from an earthquake.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/Ernest_Rutherford_%289615659875%29.jpg/250px-Ernest_Rutherford_%289615659875%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ernest_Rutherford_(9615659875).jpg)

The original Chemistry and Physics Laboratory at Canterbury College, Christchurch, NZ. Rutherford worked here as a student under the late Prof. Bickerton.

By 2013, the university had lost 22 per cent of its students.[[24]](#cite_note-24) However, a record number of 886 PhD students were enrolled at the University of Canterbury as of 2013.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Other New Zealand universities, apparently defying an informal agreement, launched billboard and print advertising campaigns in the earthquake-ravaged city to recruit University of Canterbury students who were finding it difficult to study there.[[26]](#cite_note-TVNZ_4404893-26) In 2013 the New Zealand Government also agreed to provide $260m to support the university's rebuild programme.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

Student numbers were steadily on the rise, with a 4.5% increase in students enrolled from 2013 to 2016. International numbers also increased, nearing pre-earthquake figures at 1,134 enrolled in 2016.[[28]](#cite_note-canterbury.ac.nz-28)

In March 2016, Vice-Chancellor Dr Rod Carr said in *[The Press](/wiki/The_Press "The Press")* newspaper: "In 2014, [students] wanted to leave Christchurch and went to Wellington, Otago and into the workforce. Now we're retaining Christchurch school leavers and we're getting our fair share of provincial students, as well as attracting greater numbers from the Auckland region." "Living on or near the UC campus, and having a lifestyle that can take you from lectures to skifields in 90 minutes or the beach in 20 minutes, is much more appealing and affordable than living in Auckland."[[29]](#cite_note-29)

In January 2017, the University of Canterbury released its campus master plan – 50 building and landscape projects proposed over three stages by 2045, the cost could exceed $2bn.[[30]](#cite_note-stuff.co.nz-30) In a comment to *The Press*, Rod Carr said that the plans were proof the university was moving away from the falling enrolments post-earthquake.[[30]](#cite_note-stuff.co.nz-30)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e0/ErnestRutherfordBuildingOpening_gobeirne.jpg/330px-ErnestRutherfordBuildingOpening_gobeirne.jpg)](/wiki/File:ErnestRutherfordBuildingOpening_gobeirne.jpg)

[New Zealand Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_New_Zealand "Prime Minister of New Zealand") [Jacinda Ardern](/wiki/Jacinda_Ardern "Jacinda Ardern") opened the Ernest Rutherford Regional Science and Innovation Centre at the University of Canterbury on 15 February 2018, home of the College of Science.

### 150th anniversary, 2023–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Canterbury&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 150th anniversary, 2023–present")]

The University of Canterbury celebrated its 150th anniversary in 2023.[[31]](#cite_note-31) In the same year the university experienced a surge in enrolments, reaching a record high of 21,361 students by late March, compared to 20,223 at the same period in the previous year. Among these figures, UC counted 19,975 domestic students, witnessing a substantial increase from the preceding year's count. Concurrently, the international student body also expanded to 1,393, marking a rise from 1,098 in the prior year. This growth in enrolment stands in contrast to a decline in domestic student numbers across all five North Island universities during this period.[[32]](#cite_note-32) According to a spokesperson for the university, in 2023 every affiliated hall was "at 100% occupancy" and "may be a record-breaker for highest number of enrolments".[[33]](#cite_note-33)

## Campuses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Canterbury&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Campuses")]

The University of Canterbury has three campuses spread throughout the city of Christchurch:

* **Ilam Campus:** The university has a main campus of 76 hectares (190 acres) at [Ilam](/wiki/Ilam,_New_Zealand "Ilam, New Zealand"), a suburb of Christchurch about 5 kilometres (3.1 mi) from the [centre of the city](/wiki/Christchurch_Central_City "Christchurch Central City"). The Ilam campus maintains three [libraries](/wiki/Library "Library") with the Central Library ([Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language "Māori language"): *Te Puna Mātauraka o Waitaha*) housed in the tallest building on campus, the 11-storey Puaka–James Hight Building. The Ilam campus is where the Faculties of Education, Health, Science, Engineering, Business, Law and Arts are based. The [University of Canterbury Students' Association](/wiki/University_of_Canterbury_Students%27_Association "University of Canterbury Students' Association") is based there in the Haere roa building. The Ilam Campus is home to cafes and restaurants as well as a pharmacy, bookshop, the UC rec centre and the UC Health centre.[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/University_of_Canterbury%2C_Christchurch%2C_New_Zealand%2C_12_June_2008_%28cropped%29.jpg/330px-University_of_Canterbury%2C_Christchurch%2C_New_Zealand%2C_12_June_2008_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:University_of_Canterbury,_Christchurch,_New_Zealand,_12_June_2008_(cropped).jpg)

  Aerial view of the Ilam Campus and Christchurch, with the campus prominently featured in the foreground.
* **Dovedale Campus:** The Dovedale Campus is 11 hectares (27 acres) and became a part of the University of Canterbury when the [Christchurch College of Education](/wiki/Christchurch_College_of_Education "Christchurch College of Education") (a specialist teacher training institution) merged on 1 January 2007.[[34]](#cite_note-34) The Dovedale campus is located adjacent to the Ilam campus and is off Dovedale avenue. The campus consists of the old Henry Field Library, The Christchurch College of English, Ilam early Learning Centre and Hayashi and Sonoda student residences. The Faculty of Education also maintains a presence here.
* **[City Campus](/wiki/Christchurch_Arts_Centre "Christchurch Arts Centre"):** The [Christchurch City](/wiki/Christchurch_Central_City "Christchurch Central City") Campus is made up of the Christchurch Arts Centre and the Manawa building which is a part of the Faculty of Health. Music and Classics are again taught from the Christchurch Arts Centre in the old chemistry building,[[35]](#cite_note-35) and within the new Manawa building in Christchurch city health and education are taught.[[36]](#cite_note-36) The city campus also includes the Teece Museum of Classical Antiquities – home of the James Logie Memorial Collection.

The university also maintains additional small campuses in [Nelson](/wiki/Nelson,_New_Zealand "Nelson, New Zealand"), [Tauranga](/wiki/Tauranga "Tauranga") and [Timaru](/wiki/Timaru "Timaru"), and teaching centres in [Greymouth](/wiki/Greymouth "Greymouth"), [New Plymouth](/wiki/New_Plymouth "New Plymouth"), [Rotorua](/wiki/Rotorua "Rotorua") and Timaru. The university has staff in regional information offices in Nelson, Timaru, and [Auckland](/wiki/Auckland "Auckland").

### Libraries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Canterbury&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Libraries")]

The UC Library was first established at Canterbury College in 1879. Today there are three libraries on campus each covering different subject areas.[[37]](#cite_note-Library_Locations-37)

**Central library**

Further information: [Puaka–James Hight Building](/wiki/Puaka%E2%80%93James_Hight_Building "Puaka–James Hight Building")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/James_Hight_Building_003.JPG/500px-James_Hight_Building_003.JPG)](/wiki/File:James_Hight_Building_003.JPG)

The 11-storey, Puaka-James Hight library at the University of Canterbury

The Central Library ([Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language "Māori language"): *Te Puna Mātauraka o Waitaha*)[[37]](#cite_note-Library_Locations-37) – is housed in the Puaka–James Hight Building that is designed in the [brutalist style architecture](/wiki/Brutalist_architecture "Brutalist architecture"). In 1974, the old city campus library moved to the Ilam campus and was housed in the newly constructed James Hight building, originally named after former Canterbury professor [James Hight](/wiki/James_Hight "James Hight").[[38]](#cite_note-38) The building was renamed Puaka-James Hight in 2014, after the brightest star in the cluster [Matariki](/wiki/Matariki "Matariki"), to reflect the growing strength of UC's relationship with Ngāi Tahu and the mana of Te Ao Māori at the heart of the university's campus.[[39]](#cite_note-39) The University of Canterbury Central Library is the largest university library in New Zealand.[[40]](#cite_note-40) The Central Library has collections of over 2 million physical items including books, archives, journals and a miscellany of other items that support research and teaching in Humanities, Social Sciences, Law, Commerce, Music, Fine Arts and Antarctic Studies.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

* The Henry Field Library (named for the New Zealand Educationalist [Henry Edward Field](/wiki/Henry_Edward_Field "Henry Edward Field")) on the old Christchurch College of Education site joined the fold when the university and Christchurch College of Education merged. However, the Education collection was incorporated into the collections within the Puaka–James Hight Building, and Henry Field is now a library store at the campus off Dovedale Avenue.[[42]](#cite_note-ReferenceB-42)
* A separate Law library was established within the James Hight building, it was then relocated to the new Law building (Mere Mere). However, after the [2011 Christchurch earthquake](/wiki/2011_Christchurch_earthquake "2011 Christchurch earthquake") it returned to the Puaka–James Hight Building and integrated into the Central Library collection. The Mere Mere Building still operates as the Law and Business Building however it is no longer home to the law library.[[42]](#cite_note-ReferenceB-42)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/UCLib_2%2C3Floor_Lobby.jpg/330px-UCLib_2%2C3Floor_Lobby.jpg)](/wiki/File:UCLib_2,3Floor_Lobby.jpg)

2nd and 3rd floor of Puaka–James Hight Building in 2025

**EPS library**

The EPS Library (Engineering and Physical Sciences Library, [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language "Māori language"): *Kā Puna Pūkahataka me te Pūtaiao*)[[37]](#cite_note-Library_Locations-37) supports research and teaching in Engineering, Forestry and Sciences.[[37]](#cite_note-Library_Locations-37) With the move to the Ilam campus, the Library was split. First the Engineering Library, and later the Physical Sciences Library, moving to the new campus however the old Physical Sciences Library closed and its collections moved to the Engineering Library now called the EPS Library.

**Macmillian Brown library**

The [Macmillan Brown Library](/wiki/Macmillan_Brown_Library "Macmillan Brown Library") ([Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language "Māori language"): *Te Puna Rakahau o Macmillan Brown*)[[37]](#cite_note-Library_Locations-37) is a research library, archive, and art gallery that specialises in collecting items related to New Zealand and [Pacific Islands](/wiki/Pacific_Islands "Pacific Islands") history.[[43]](#cite_note-library.canterbury.ac.nz-43)[[44]](#cite_note-Heritage_Collections-44) It holds over 100,000 published items including books, audio-visual recordings, and various manuscripts, photographs, works of art, architectural drawings and ephemera. The Macmillan Brown Library's art collection also has over 5,000 works, making it one of the largest collections in the [Canterbury Region](/wiki/Canterbury_Region "Canterbury Region").[[45]](#cite_note-45) Some notable items in its collections include copies of Māori Land Court Records, official and government documents from various Pacific Islands states, trade union records, and the personal papers of various [Members of Parliament](/wiki/Members_of_Parliament "Members of Parliament") and government ministers. The library is named after [John Macmillan Brown](/wiki/John_Macmillan_Brown "John Macmillan Brown"), a prominent Canterbury academic who helped found the library, allocated a large proportion of his fortune to the Macmillan Brown Library.[[43]](#cite_note-library.canterbury.ac.nz-43)[[44]](#cite_note-Heritage_Collections-44)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Ilam_Apartments_in_2019.jpg/330px-Ilam_Apartments_in_2019.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ilam_Apartments_in_2019.jpg)

### Student accommodation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Canterbury&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Student accommodation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Sonoda_Hall_Grounds%2C_University_of_Canterbury.jpg/330px-Sonoda_Hall_Grounds%2C_University_of_Canterbury.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sonoda_Hall_Grounds,_University_of_Canterbury.jpg)

The grounds at Sonoda, taken in October 2019

The university has ten student residences throughout its Ilam and Dovedale campuses: five fully-catered halls of residence exclusively for first-year undergraduate students: Arcady, College House, Rochester and Rutherford, Tupuānuku and University Hall; and five other self-catered student accommodation houses which are home to both undergraduate and postgraduate students: Sonoda Christchurch Campus, Hayashi, Kirkwood Avenue, Waimairi Village and Ilam Apartments. The largest, Ilam Apartments, houses 831 students during the academic year.[[46]](#cite_note-46) Some of the halls at UC have storied histories; Tupuānuku is named for the star of the same name that is connected to food grown in the ground in the cluster Matariki in [Māori Mythology](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_mythology "Māori mythology");[[47]](#cite_note-47) Rochester and Rutherford is named for former alumni Ernest Rutherford and [John Fisher Bishop of Rochester](/wiki/John_Fisher "John Fisher"); while Arcady, previously Bishop Julius Hall, was founded by the first Archbishop of New Zealand, [Churchill Julius](/wiki/Churchill_Julius "Churchill Julius");[[48]](#cite_note-48) additionally, [College House](/wiki/College_House_(University_of_Canterbury) "College House (University of Canterbury)") is the oldest residential college in New Zealand.[[49]](#cite_note-College_House_history-49)[[50]](#cite_note-Housing_&_Orientation-50)

The University also has a new hall, Tupuārangi, planned for completion in 2026. At Tupuārangi, every room will have its own ensuite.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

### Field facilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Canterbury&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Field facilities")]

The University of Canterbury has the most field stations of any New Zealand university.[[52]](#cite_note-52) The Field Facilities Centre[[53]](#cite_note-53) administers four of these field stations:

* [Cass Field Station](/wiki/Cass_Field_Station "Cass Field Station")[[54]](#cite_note-54) – Established in 1914 to give students and researchs access to [montane grasslands](/wiki/Southland_montane_grasslands "Southland montane grasslands"), scrub, riverbed, scree, beech forest, swamp, bog, lake, stream and alpine habitats.
* Harihari Field Station[[55]](#cite_note-55) – Access to native forests, streams.
* Westport Field Station[[56]](#cite_note-56) – for study of the [West Coast](/wiki/West_Coast,_New_Zealand "West Coast, New Zealand") of New Zealand, particularly mining.
* Kaikōura Field Station[[57]](#cite_note-57) – [Kaikōura](/wiki/Kaik%C5%8Dura_District "Kaikōura District") represents an important transition zone for flora and fauna, particularly in the marine environment, with Kowhai bush and associated rich bird life close by.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Mount_John_University_Observatory_%289618676129%29.jpg/250px-Mount_John_University_Observatory_%289618676129%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mount_John_University_Observatory_(9618676129).jpg)

Mount John University of Canterbury Observatory, opened 1965

The university and its project partners also operate an additional field station in the Nigerian Montane Forests Project;[[58]](#cite_note-58) this field station stands on the Ngel Nyaki forest edge in Nigeria.

The Department of Physical and Chemical Sciences runs its own field laboratories:

* [Mount John University Observatory](/wiki/Mount_John_University_Observatory "Mount John University Observatory") at [Lake Tekapo](/wiki/Lake_Tekapo_(town) "Lake Tekapo (town)") for optical astronomical research[[59]](#cite_note-59)
* [Birdlings Flat](/wiki/Birdlings_Flat "Birdlings Flat") [radar](/wiki/Radar "Radar") facility[[60]](#cite_note-60)
* [Scott Base](/wiki/Scott_Base "Scott Base") radar facility[[61]](#cite_note-61)
* [Cracroft Caverns](/wiki/Cracroft_Caverns "Cracroft Caverns") ring [laser](/wiki/Laser "Laser") facility[[62]](#cite_note-62)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Scott_Base%2C_Antarctica%2C_Jan_2006.jpg/250px-Scott_Base%2C_Antarctica%2C_Jan_2006.jpg)](/wiki/File:Scott_Base,_Antarctica,_Jan_2006.jpg)

Scott Base, used by University of Canterbury postgraduate Antarctic studies students.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

The Department of Physical and Chemical Sciences also has involvement in the [Southern African Large Telescope](/wiki/Southern_African_Large_Telescope "Southern African Large Telescope")[[64]](#cite_note-64) and is a member of the IceCube collaboration which is installing a neutrino telescope at the South Pole.[[65]](#cite_note-65)[[66]](#cite_note-66)

### Teece Museum of Classical Antiquities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Canterbury&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Teece Museum of Classical Antiquities")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Central_Art_Gallery_in_Christchurch_09.jpg/250px-Central_Art_Gallery_in_Christchurch_09.jpg)](/wiki/File:Central_Art_Gallery_in_Christchurch_09.jpg)

Clock Tower, Great Hall and Classics buildings in the North Quad

The University of Canterbury Teece Museum of Classical Antiquities opened in May 2017, and showcases the James Logie Memorial Collection, a collection of [Greek](/wiki/Classical_archaeology "Classical archaeology"), [Roman](/wiki/Roman_Archaeology "Roman Archaeology"), [Egyptian](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt") and [Near Eastern](/wiki/Near_Eastern_archaeology "Near Eastern archaeology") artefacts in New Zealand.[[67]](#cite_note-67) The Teece Museum is run as a part of the faculty of Arts. The museum is named for University of Canterbury Alumni Professor [David Teece](/wiki/David_Teece "David Teece") and his wife Leigh Teece, who donated a substantial amount of money to the city for earthquake recovery. The money was used by the university to install the classics and music school in the Old Chemistry building at the [Christchurch Arts Centre](/wiki/Christchurch_Arts_Centre "Christchurch Arts Centre").[[68]](#cite_note-68)

The James Logie Memorial Collection was established in 1957 as a result of Miss Marion Steven, a Classics faculty member, donating Greek pottery to Canterbury University College. Steven established the James Logie Memorial Collection to honour her husband, who served as registrar of the college from 1950 until his death in 1956.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

The Logie Collection includes a wide range of pottery, beginning with the [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age") cultures of [Cyprus](/wiki/Ancient_cyprus "Ancient cyprus"), [Crete](/wiki/Ancient_Crete "Ancient Crete") and [Mycenae](/wiki/Mycenaean_Greece "Mycenaean Greece") it also includes vases that come from [Corinth](/wiki/Ancient_Corinth "Ancient Corinth") and [Athens](/wiki/Ancient_Athens "Ancient Athens"), the islands in the [Aegean](/wiki/Aegean_Sea "Aegean Sea"), East Greece and the Greek colonies in South Italy and [Sicily](/wiki/Greek_Sicily "Greek Sicily").[[70]](#cite_note-70)

## Organisation and administration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Canterbury&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Organisation and administration")]

### Governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Canterbury&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Governance")]

See also the categories [Chancellors of the University of Canterbury](/wiki/Category:Chancellors_of_the_University_of_Canterbury "Category:Chancellors of the University of Canterbury") and [Vice-chancellors of the University of Canterbury](/wiki/Category:Vice-chancellors_of_the_University_of_Canterbury "Category:Vice-chancellors of the University of Canterbury")

The university was first governed by a board of governors (1873–1933), then by a college council (1933–1957), and since 1957 by a university council.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGardner_et_al1973451-71) The council is chaired by a [chancellor](/wiki/Chancellor_(education) "Chancellor (education)").[[72]](#cite_note-72) The Council includes representatives from the faculties, students and general staff, as well as local industry, employer and trade union representatives.[[73]](#cite_note-73)

The original composition of the board of governors was defined in the Canterbury College Ordinance 1873,[[74]](#cite_note-74) which was passed by the [Canterbury Provincial Council](/wiki/Canterbury_Provincial_Council "Canterbury Provincial Council") and named 23 members who might serve for life. Initially, the board was given power to fill their own vacancies, and this power transferred to graduates once their number exceeded 30.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGardner_et_al197338–39-75) At the time, there were discussions about the abolition of provincial government (which did happen in 1876), and the governance structure was set up to give board members "prestige, power and permanence", and "provincial authority and its membership and resources were safely perpetuated, beyond the reach of grasping hands in Wellington."[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGardner_et_al197339-76)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Matariki_Building%2C_University_of_Canterbury.jpg/250px-Matariki_Building%2C_University_of_Canterbury.jpg)](/wiki/File:Matariki_Building,_University_of_Canterbury.jpg)

The Matariki Building (Registry Building) serves as home of the University Council which is responsible for the university's governance.

Original members of the Board of Governors were:[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGardner_et_al1973452-77) [Charles Bowen](/wiki/Charles_Bowen_(New_Zealand_politician) "Charles Bowen (New Zealand politician)"), Rev James Buller,[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScholefield1940a118-78)[[79]](#cite_note-79)[[80]](#cite_note-80) William Patten Cowlishaw,[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScholefield1940a179-81) [John Enys](/wiki/John_Enys_(naturalist) "John Enys (naturalist)"),[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScholefield1940a231f-82) [Charles Fraser](/wiki/Charles_Fraser_(minister) "Charles Fraser (minister)"), George Gould Sr,[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScholefield1940a311f-83) [Henry Barnes Gresson](/wiki/Henry_Barnes_Gresson "Henry Barnes Gresson"),[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScholefield1940a322f-84) [William Habens](/wiki/William_Habens "William Habens"), [John Hall](/wiki/John_Hall_(New_Zealand_politician) "John Hall (New Zealand politician)"), [Henry Harper](/wiki/Henry_Harper_(bishop) "Henry Harper (bishop)"), John Inglis,[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScholefield1940a426-85) [Walter Kennaway](/wiki/Walter_Kennaway "Walter Kennaway"),[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScholefield1940a459-86) Arthur C. Knight,[[87]](#cite_note-87) Thomas William Maude,[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScholefield1940b74f-88) [William Montgomery](/wiki/William_Montgomery_(New_Zealand_politician) "William Montgomery (New Zealand politician)"), [Thomas Potts](/wiki/Thomas_Potts_(naturalist) "Thomas Potts (naturalist)"), [William Rolleston](/wiki/William_Rolleston "William Rolleston"), [John Studholme](/wiki/John_Studholme "John Studholme"), [Henry Tancred](/wiki/Henry_Tancred_(New_Zealand_politician) "Henry Tancred (New Zealand politician)"), James Somerville Turnbull,[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEScholefield1940b404-89) [Henry Richard Webb](/wiki/Henry_Richard_Webb "Henry Richard Webb"), [Joshua Williams](/wiki/Joshua_Williams_(lawyer) "Joshua Williams (lawyer)"), and Rev William Wellington Willock.[[90]](#cite_note-90)

Professor Roy Sharp assumed the position of [Vice-Chancellor](/wiki/Vice-Chancellor "Vice-Chancellor") on 1 March 2003.[[91]](#cite_note-comsdev.canterbury.ac.nz-91) In May 2008 he announced his imminent resignation from the position, following his acceptance of the chief executive position at the [Tertiary Education Commission](/wiki/Tertiary_Education_Commission_(New_Zealand) "Tertiary Education Commission (New Zealand)") (TEC)[[92]](#cite_note-92)[[93]](#cite_note-93) which he took up on 4 August 2008.[[94]](#cite_note-94) The then current Deputy Vice-Chancellor, Professor Ian Town, assumed the role of acting Vice-Chancellor on 1 July 2008. On 15 October 2008 the university announced that [Rod Carr](/wiki/Rod_Carr_(administrator) "Rod Carr (administrator)"), a former banker and the CEO of a local software company, would begin a five-year appointment as Vice-Chancellor on 1 February 2009.[[95]](#cite_note-95)

Council member and former Pro-Chancellor, Rex Williams, became Chancellor in 2009.[[96]](#cite_note-Wood_appointment-96) Council member [John Wood](/wiki/John_Wood_(diplomat) "John Wood (diplomat)") became the new Pro-Chancellor. On 1 January 2012, Wood became Chancellor after Williams retired from the role.[[96]](#cite_note-Wood_appointment-96) In 2019, a new Vice Chancellor, [Cheryl de la Rey](/wiki/Cheryl_de_la_Rey "Cheryl de la Rey"), was appointed from the [University of Pretoria](/wiki/University_of_Pretoria "University of Pretoria"), and [Susan McCormack](/wiki/Susan_McCormack "Susan McCormack") took over as Chancellor.[[97]](#cite_note-97)[[98]](#cite_note-98)

As of 2026 the University of Canterbury is divided into seven faculties, which encompass all major departments of both research and teaching.

### Coat of arms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Canterbury&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Coat of arms")]

Coat of arms of the University of Canterbury

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | Notes  With the dissolution of the University of New Zealand, the newly independent University of Canterbury devised its own coat of arms, blazoned  Escutcheon  Murrey a fleece argent, in base a plough or, and on a chief wavy or an open book proper bound murrey, edged and clasped or between a pall azure charged with four crosses formy fitchy or and a cross flory azure. |

The fleece symbolises the [pastoral](/wiki/Pastoralism "Pastoralism"), and the plough at the base the [agricultural](/wiki/Agricultural "Agricultural") background of the [province of Canterbury](/wiki/Canterbury_Province "Canterbury Province"). The bishop's [pall](/wiki/Pall_(heraldry) "Pall (heraldry)") and the [cross flory](/wiki/Cross_flory "Cross flory") represent Canterbury's [ecclesiastical](/wiki/Ecclesiastical "Ecclesiastical") connections, and the open book denotes scholarship.[[114]](#cite_note-114) As an institution of learning, the university's coat of arms does not have a helmet, crest or [mantling](/wiki/Mantling "Mantling"). The university's unofficial coat of arms was accompanied by the Latin [motto](/wiki/Motto "Motto"):

> Ergo tua rura manebunt (therefore the lands shall remain yours).

— Roman Poet [Virgil](/wiki/Virgil "Virgil"), [Eclogue I](/wiki/Eclogue_1 "Eclogue 1"), line 46

Because of the land holdings with which the Provincial Government [endowed](/wiki/Financial_endowment "Financial endowment") the early University, this was appropriate. When the coat of arms was redesigned, the motto was removed and now the motto is only used unofficially.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/7308_Old_University_Coat_of_Arms.jpg/250px-7308_Old_University_Coat_of_Arms.jpg)](/wiki/File:7308_Old_University_Coat_of_Arms.jpg)

An Oamaru stone carving of the university coat of arms can be viewed at the eastern entrance of the Matariki Building (Registry Building).

## Academic profile

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Canterbury&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Academic profile")]

The University of Canterbury offers 147 [undergraduate](/wiki/Undergraduate_degree "Undergraduate degree") majors[[115]](#cite_note-115) and 61 graduate degrees.[[116]](#cite_note-116)[[117]](#cite_note-117)  For the 2020 academic year, the university granted 2,257 bachelor's degrees, 1,003 graduate degrees, and 384 [honours degrees](/wiki/Honours_degree "Honours degree").[[2]](#cite_note-annualreport-2) To graduate with a full-time undergraduate degree in the usual three years, undergraduates normally take four courses per semester. In most majors, an honours degree requires advanced coursework and a thesis – this usually takes an extra year. However, some undergraduate degrees that are also [professional degrees](/wiki/Professional_degree "Professional degree"), such as the [Bachelor of Laws](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Laws "Bachelor of Laws") (LLB), [Bachelor of Engineering](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Engineering "Bachelor of Engineering") (BEng) and Bachelor of Forestry Science (BForSc), typically take four years.

### Size and composition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Canterbury&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Size and composition")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/UoC_graduation_April_2014_21.JPG/250px-UoC_graduation_April_2014_21.JPG)](/wiki/File:UoC_graduation_April_2014_21.JPG)

University of Canterbury graduation ceremony held at [Christchurch Town Hall](/wiki/Christchurch_Town_Hall "Christchurch Town Hall").

According to the UC Annual Report, at 31 December 2024 the university has a total of 26,433 students (18,319 equivalent full-time students), up 9% on the previous year. 2,587 are Maori students. UC has a total of 2,484 staff members.[[118]](#cite_note-118)

Following the earthquakes, the number of students enrolled at UC fell from 18,783 during 2010 to 14,725 during 2014, though the number of new enrolments increased in 2014. In 2016 enrolled student numbers rose to 15,564.[[28]](#cite_note-canterbury.ac.nz-28) Enrolment as of 2020 has reached pre-earthquake levels with a 18,364 students enrolled at UC.

## Student life

### Students' association

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Canterbury&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Students' association")]

Main article: [University of Canterbury Students' Association](/wiki/University_of_Canterbury_Students%27_Association "University of Canterbury Students' Association")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Haere-roa_entrance_%28Ilam_road%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/330px-Haere-roa_entrance_%28Ilam_road%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Haere-roa_entrance_(Ilam_road)_(cropped).jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Haere-roa_lawn_%28cropped%29.jpg/330px-Haere-roa_lawn_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Haere-roa_lawn_(cropped).jpg)

Haere-roa (the UCSA building)

There are two main student organisations within the University of Canterbury, University of Canterbury Students' Association (UCSA) and Te Akatoki Māori Students' Association.[[136]](#cite_note-137) The UCSA is based in Haere-roa, the university’s student union building and main hub for [student activity](/wiki/Student_activity_center "Student activity center") on the Ilam Campus. The original UCSA Building was damaged in the 2010 and 2011 earthquakes and was subsequently torn down, it was rebuilt and completed in 2019. Haere-roa is home to the student bar, The Foundry, and the Ngaio Marsh Theatre, named for the former alumnus of the [same name](/wiki/Ngaio_Marsh "Ngaio Marsh"). Haere-roa hosts a number of student societies and organisation offices. The USCA also runs several cafes and restaurants around campus.[[137]](#cite_note-138)

The university's student population operates the main [student magazine](/wiki/Student_magazine "Student magazine"), *[Canta](/wiki/Canta_(magazine) "Canta (magazine)")*, which was established in 1930. There are 12 issues per year, which are distributed around the UC campus every second Monday during the academic year.[[138]](#cite_note-139) The newspaper's offices are in Haere-roa.

Canterbury's student population also runs a radio station which began to broadcast and operate as [RDU](/wiki/RDU-FM "RDU-FM") in 1976;[[139]](#cite_note-140) it began [FM frequency](/wiki/FM_broadcasting "FM broadcasting") broadcasting in 1986. RDU acquired its present frequency of 98.5 in 2003.

### Clubs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Canterbury&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Clubs")]

The university has over 140 academic, sporting, recreational and cultural societies and clubs.[[143]](#cite_note-147) The most prominent of these include the [Student Volunteer Army](/wiki/Student_Volunteer_Army "Student Volunteer Army"), the [University of Canterbury Engineering Society (ENSOC)](/wiki/University_of_Canterbury_Engineering_Society_Inc._(ENSOC) "University of Canterbury Engineering Society Inc. (ENSOC)"), the University of Canterbury Law Society (LAWSOC), the University of Canterbury Commerce Society (UCOM) as well as the largest non-faculty clubs such as PongSoc (University of Canterbury Pong Society), Motosoc (Motorsports Society), Lads without Labels, CUBA (Canterbury University Boardriders' Association), CUTC (Tramping Club), UC Global Society (UCGS), UC Bike, Opsoc, The Gentlemen's Club. CUSSC (Canterbury University Snow Sports Club, formerly the CU Ski Club prior to 1997) is the only university club in New Zealand to own a ski field lodge, located at [Temple Basin Ski Field](/wiki/Temple_Basin "Temple Basin").[[144]](#cite_note-148) The club runs many events to raise funds for maintenance of their lodge. The [University of Canterbury Drama Society](/wiki/University_of_Canterbury_Drama_Society "University of Canterbury Drama Society") (Dramasoc) achieved fame for its 1942–1969 Shakespeare productions under Dame [Ngaio Marsh](/wiki/Ngaio_Marsh "Ngaio Marsh"), but regularly performs as an active student- and alumni-run arts fixture in the small Christchurch theatre-scene.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Ensoc.jpg/250px-Ensoc.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ensoc.jpg)

Engineering Society (ENSOC) Logo.

In 2011 the SVA was established in the aftermath of the [Christchurch earthquakes](/wiki/2011_Christchurch_earthquake "2011 Christchurch earthquake"). A student at the University of Canterbury, [Sam Johnson](/wiki/Sam_Johnson_(activist) "Sam Johnson (activist)"), rallied fellow students to help support the clean-up from the devastation. The club has grown and today is the largest club at the University of Canterbury.[[145]](#cite_note-149) In 2020, in response to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_New_Zealand "COVID-19 pandemic in New Zealand"), the SVA supported the vulnerable with their shopping during the lockdown.[[146]](#cite_note-150)[[147]](#cite_note-151)

Lads without Labels is a not-for-profit charity dedicated to improving men's mental health in and around campus. On 5–8 October 2021 Lads without labels started the Project 72 fundraiser, a 72-hour relay on the University of Canterbury campus, which raised $12,000 for men's mental health; as well in 2021 Lads without labels organised a 24-hour backyard cricket marathon raising $45,000.[[148]](#cite_note-152)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Undie500Noah.jpg/250px-Undie500Noah.jpg)](/wiki/File:Undie500Noah.jpg)

An [Undie 500](/wiki/Undie_500 "Undie 500") car decorated as [Noah's Ark](/wiki/Noah%27s_Ark "Noah's Ark")

One major student tradition, the [Undie 500](/wiki/Undie_500_Car_Rally "Undie 500 Car Rally"), involved an annual car-rally from Christchurch to [Dunedin](/wiki/Dunedin "Dunedin") run by [ENSOC](/wiki/University_of_Canterbury_Engineering_Society_Inc._(ENSOC) "University of Canterbury Engineering Society Inc. (ENSOC)"). The rules required only the use of a road-legal car costing under [$](/wiki/New_Zealand_dollar "New Zealand dollar")500 with a sober driver.[[149]](#cite_note-153) The 2007 event gained international news coverage (including on [CNN](/wiki/CNN "CNN") and [BBC World](/wiki/BBC_World "BBC World")) when it ended in rioting in the student quarter of [Dunedin](/wiki/Dunedin "Dunedin") and in [North East Valley](/wiki/North_East_Valley "North East Valley"). ENSOC cancelled the planned 2008 event. The Undie 500 was replaced by the Roundie 500 in 2011. This event has the same principles but follows a route through rural Canterbury, returning to Christchurch the same day.[[150]](#cite_note-154)

Judges who attended Canterbury

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/William_Young_2020_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-William_Young_2020_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:William_Young_2020_(cropped).jpg)

William Young

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Michael_Myers%2C_New_Zealand.jpg/120px-Michael_Myers%2C_New_Zealand.jpg)](/wiki/File:Michael_Myers,_New_Zealand.jpg)

Michael Myers

Canterbury has produced a large number of distinguished [jurists](/wiki/Jurist "Jurist"), judges and lawyers around the world. Among its alumni are [Supreme Court Justices](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_New_Zealand "Supreme Court of New Zealand") [Andrew Tipping](/wiki/Andrew_Tipping "Andrew Tipping"), New Zealand's longest-serving judge, who spent 25 years on the bench, and [William Young](/wiki/William_Young_(judge) "William Young (judge)"), also former [President of the Court of Appeal](/wiki/Court_of_Appeal_of_New_Zealand "Court of Appeal of New Zealand").[[161]](#cite_note-165) [Ivor Richardson](/wiki/Ivor_Richardson "Ivor Richardson"), also a former President of the Court of Appeal, is credited with significantly influencing the development of New Zealand tax law and policy.[[162]](#cite_note-166)

Furthermore, Canterbury has educated six current [High Court Judges](/wiki/High_Court_of_New_Zealand "High Court of New Zealand"), including former Chief High Court Judge [Geoffrey Venning](/wiki/Geoffrey_Venning "Geoffrey Venning").[[163]](#cite_note-167) Other notable Lawyers include: [Matthew Palmer](/wiki/Matthew_Palmer "Matthew Palmer") Justice of the Court of Appeal (though he only completed his B.A. at Canterbury);[[164]](#cite_note-168) [Graham Panckhurst](/wiki/Graham_Panckhurst "Graham Panckhurst") Justice of the High Court and chair of the Royal Commission into the [Pike River Mine disaster](/wiki/Pike_River_Mine_disaster "Pike River Mine disaster"); and [Michael Myers](/wiki/Michael_Myers_(judge) "Michael Myers (judge)") sixth [Chief Justice of New Zealand](/wiki/Chief_Justice_of_New_Zealand "Chief Justice of New Zealand").[[165]](#cite_note-169) The New Zealand Government's [Attorneys General](/wiki/Attorney-General_(New_Zealand) "Attorney-General (New Zealand)") have included: Judith Collins and Michael Cullen.

Don Brash

One of the most notable Canterbury alumni is [Ernest Rutherford](/wiki/Ernest_Rutherford "Ernest Rutherford") a physicist described as "the father of nuclear physics", and who features on the [New Zealand one hundred-dollar note](/wiki/New_Zealand_one_hundred-dollar_note "New Zealand one hundred-dollar note"). In 1908 he was awarded the [Nobel Prize in Chemistry](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Chemistry "Nobel Prize in Chemistry") "for his investigations into the disintegration of the elements, and the chemistry of radioactive substances". In 1917 Rutherford "split the atom".[[166]](#cite_note-170) [Roy Kerr](/wiki/Roy_Kerr "Roy Kerr"), was also educated at Canterbury, he discovered the [Kerr Solution](/wiki/Kerr_Solution "Kerr Solution"), an exact solution to the [Einstein field equation of general relativity](/wiki/Einstein_field_equations "Einstein field equations").[[167]](#cite_note-171) [Beatrice Tinsley](/wiki/Beatrice_Tinsley "Beatrice Tinsley") an [astronomer](/wiki/Astronomer "Astronomer") and [cosmologist](/wiki/Cosmologist "Cosmologist") who through her research in the United States in the 1960s and 1970s, proved that the universe was infinite and would expand forever.[[168]](#cite_note-172) [Richard Barrer](/wiki/Richard_Barrer "Richard Barrer") was a 'founding father' of [zeolite](/wiki/Zeolite "Zeolite") science and its applications he gave his name to the zeolite [Barrerite](/wiki/Barrerite "Barrerite"), and the [barrer](/wiki/Barrer "Barrer"), a unit of gas permeability.[[169]](#cite_note-173) Other notable scientists include: [Ian Axford](/wiki/Ian_Axford "Ian Axford"), [Toby Hendy](/wiki/Toby_Hendy "Toby Hendy"), [Ian Foster](/wiki/Ian_Foster_(computer_scientist) "Ian Foster (computer scientist)"), [Craig Nevill-Manning](/wiki/Craig_Nevill-Manning "Craig Nevill-Manning") and [Michelle Rogan-Finnemore](/wiki/Michelle_Rogan-Finnemore "Michelle Rogan-Finnemore").

Notable [engineers](/wiki/Engineer "Engineer") include: [John Britten](/wiki/John_Britten "John Britten"), a [mechanical engineer](/wiki/Mechanical_engineering "Mechanical engineering"), who designed the [Britten motorcycle](/wiki/Britten_Motorcycle_Company "Britten Motorcycle Company") that won races and set numerous speed records on international circuits;[[170]](#cite_note-174) and [Bill Pickering](/wiki/Bill_Pickering_(rocket_scientist) "Bill Pickering (rocket scientist)"), an [aerospace engineer](/wiki/Aerospace_engineering "Aerospace engineering"), who headed Pasadena, California's [Jet Propulsion Laboratory](/wiki/Jet_Propulsion_Laboratory "Jet Propulsion Laboratory") for 22 years.[[171]](#cite_note-175) Other notable engineers include [Nigel Priestley](/wiki/Nigel_Priestley "Nigel Priestley"), [Thomas Paulay](/wiki/Thomas_Paulay "Thomas Paulay") and [David Beauchamp](/wiki/David_Beauchamp "David Beauchamp").

Economists [Rex Bergstrom](/wiki/Rex_Bergstrom "Rex Bergstrom"), [Brian Easton](/wiki/Brian_Easton_(economist) "Brian Easton (economist)"), [Ken Henry](/wiki/Ken_Henry_(public_servant) "Ken Henry (public servant)"), [Graham Scott](/wiki/Graham_Scott_(public_servant) "Graham Scott (public servant)") and [John McMillan](/wiki/John_McMillan_(economist) "John McMillan (economist)") attended Canterbury. Don Brash, who received a [B.A.](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Arts "Bachelor of Arts") and [M.A.](/wiki/Master_of_Arts "Master of Arts") in economics, also served as [Governor of the Reserve Bank](/wiki/Reserve_Bank_of_New_Zealand "Reserve Bank of New Zealand"). He presided over the reforms of central-bank autonomy and accountability under the Public Finance Act 1989 which were described as "genuine innovations".[[172]](#cite_note-176)

Since 1962, the University of Canterbury has been awarding [honorary doctorates](/wiki/Honorary_doctorate "Honorary doctorate"). In many years, no awards were made, but in most years, multiple doctorates were awarded. The highest number of honorary doctorates was awarded in 1973, when there were seven recipients.[[177]](#cite_note-181)

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Canterbury&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Ilam_Homestead_6308_Staff_Club.jpg/330px-Ilam_Homestead_6308_Staff_Club.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ilam_Homestead_6308_Staff_Club.jpg)

The Ilam Homestead, home of the University of Canterbury Staff Club. The home of [Juliet Hulme](/wiki/Anne_Perry "Anne Perry"), during the 1950s.

* *[Heavenly Creatures](/wiki/Heavenly_Creatures "Heavenly Creatures")* (1994) – Based on the [Parker–Hulme murder case](/wiki/Parker%E2%80%93Hulme_murder_case "Parker–Hulme murder case"), [Juliet Hulme](/wiki/Anne_Perry "Anne Perry") (later known as Anne Perry) was the daughter of [Henry Hulme](/wiki/Henry_Rainsford_Hulme "Henry Rainsford Hulme"), the [rector](/wiki/Rector_(academia) "Rector (academia)") of the University of Canterbury, and she and Pauline Parker murdered Honorah Parker, Pauline's mother. During this period Juliet and her family lived on campus in the Ilam homestead (now the University of Canterbury Staff Club), where filming for the movie took place.[[178]](#cite_note-182)
